FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,066, baseline ages 9-10 years, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, analyzed ABCD 3.0 release

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis with longitudinal design (baseline 2016-2018, 1-year follow-up 2017-2019)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in Pediatric Obesity, 2021

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time modalities measured: texting, video chat, video games, TV, videos, social networking. Individual-trait outcome: BMI percentile (health/physical variable). Screen time is IV, BMI is DV. Methods: "Children answered questions about typical hours per day spent on six different screen modalities..."

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses with reported β coefficients: texting β=0.92 (95% CI 0.29-1.55, p=0.004), video chat β=0.72 (95% CI 0.09-1.36, p=0.025), video games β=0.42 (95% CI 0.06-0.78, p=0.021)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results reported: fully adjusted regression models show associations between screen time and BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up with p-values <0.05

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is IV (exposure), BMI percentile is DV (outcome). Design is longitudinal but EC7 requires SM as DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis. This paper has SM as IV, so EC7 does not apply.
notes: High-quality prospective analysis of contemporary screen time modalities with clear SM/phone measurement and individual-trait outcome (BMI). Meets all criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,875 participants, ages 9-10 years at baseline, nationwide sample from 21 recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with cross-sectional design (baseline data only). Correlation and regression analyses conducted examining relationships between screen time and multiple outcome variables

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in PLOS ONE, 2021

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured via Screen Time Questionnaire across modalities: TV/movies, videos, video games, texting, video chat, social networking. Individual-trait outcomes include: depression/anxiety symptoms, behavioral problems (externalizing, internalizing, ODD, conduct disorder, ADHD), academic performance, sleep (quantity and quality), peer relationships (number of close friends). Multiple analyses pair each screen time type with individual-trait variables as outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Bivariate Pearson correlations and multiple regression analyses with reported results. Table 1 shows correlations between screen time measures and outcomes. Table 2 shows standardized beta coefficients and t-statistics for main effects of screen time and sex on depression, anxiety, internalizing, externalizing, oppositional defiance, conduct disorder, attention problems, and ADHD

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results throughout: correlation coefficients, standardized beta coefficients, t-statistics, p-values. Example: main effect of screen time on depression, main effect of screen time on anxiety, etc. with p-values reported

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (not longitudinal). FT-EC7 explicitly does not apply to cross-sectional studies regardless of which side SM appears.
notes: Large diverse sample, multiple outcome variables representing individual-trait constructs (mental health, behavioral, academic, sleep, social). Extensive inferential analyses with reported numeric results across multiple tests.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study subsample pilot project. Participants aged 11-12 years from ABCD Study sites. Four ABCD study sites involved in passive sensing substudy.

FT_IC5.evidence: This is a methodology/validation study of passive sensing technology (EARS app). Primary focus is feasibility, acceptability, and concordance between self-report and objective measurement. Does not pair passive smartphone measurement with an external individual-trait-level outcome variable. The study examines app use types (streaming, communication, gaming, social media) descriptively but does not conduct inferential analyses linking smartphone/social media use to a behavioral, mental health, neural, or other individual-trait variable. This is fundamentally a validation/methodology paper rather than an empirical analysis of associations.

notes: This paper describes the development and validation of a passive sensing protocol for measuring smartphone app use. While it measures smartphone/app data, it does not pair these measurements with individual-trait outcomes (no mental health, behavioral, cognitive, or health analyses). The results section reports descriptive statistics on app use categories and feasibility/acceptability. This is a resource/methodology paper, not an empirical analysis paper linking SM/phone to individual traits. Fails FT-IC5.
